content.text: A platform SLA (service level agreement) defines the reliability and response commitments the AI developer platform team makes to its internal customers. A support model defines how developers get help when things go wrong. Together, they transform the platform from an informal infrastructure project into a product that engineering teams can build against with confidence.
What it is

An internal platform SLA is a formal commitment, typically expressed as a Notion or Confluence page, that specifies availability targets (e.g., 99.9% uptime for the LLM gateway), latency targets (e.g., p99 API response under 3 seconds), and response time commitments for incidents or support requests. A support model defines how requests reach the platform team — which channels, which tiers, and what response time each tier receives.

Unlike vendor SLAs, internal platform SLAs are not legally binding contracts. Their power comes from visibility and expectation-setting: developers know what they can rely on, and the platform team is accountable to a published standard. A missed SLA commitment should trigger a postmortem, just as a production incident would.
Why it matters

Without a defined SLA, developers have no baseline for what platform reliability should look like. They either over-rely on the platform for use cases where its reliability is insufficient, or they under-invest in the platform because they have no confidence it can meet their needs. Either outcome produces shadow infrastructure: direct vendor API calls, custom rate limit handlers, and bespoke failover logic that fragments the organization's observability posture.

A support model matters for a complementary reason: when something breaks, developers need to know where to go for help. Without a defined model, help-seeking defaults to the loudest Slack thread or whoever has the engineering manager's attention. This creates inequitable access and consumes platform team time on ad-hoc triage rather than systematic fixes.
Benefits
Trust foundation: a published SLA gives developers the confidence to build production systems on the platform rather than maintaining fallback vendor integrations.
Accountability mechanism: a missed SLA triggers a postmortem and corrective action, preventing reliability regressions from accumulating silently.
Capacity clarity: tiered support models make the cost of different response time commitments explicit, enabling the platform team to staff support appropriately.
Incident prioritization: SLA tier assignments give on-call engineers a clear framework for triaging which incidents require immediate response versus next-day investigation.
Stakeholder communication: during incidents, the SLA defines what information to provide and when — reducing ad-hoc stakeholder escalations.
Procurement input: when evaluating upstream LLM vendor SLAs, the platform team's own internal SLAs define the minimum vendor SLA required to keep commitments to developers.
Trade-offs and limitations

Commitment risk: a published SLA creates accountability. If the platform lacks the observability, alerting, or on-call infrastructure to reliably detect and respond to SLA breaches, publishing commitments before the infrastructure is ready creates more trust erosion than no SLA.
Tier boundary disputes: developers with Tier 2 use cases will attempt to escalate to Tier 1 response times during incidents. Clear tier assignment criteria are needed.
Maintenance overhead: SLAs must be reviewed and updated when the platform architecture changes. A stale SLA that commits to unreachable targets is worse than none.
False precision: committing to four nines (99.99%) requires a different operational posture than three nines. Start with commitments you can actually deliver.
Incentive misalignment: a support model that measures response time can create perverse incentives to send a quick acknowledgment rather than actually solving the problem.
How it works

Define three service tiers mapped to business criticality. Tier 1 covers production-critical agentic services that directly affect customer revenue or regulatory commitments — these get 24x7 on-call response with a 1-hour initial response target. Tier 2 covers standard internal developer tooling and staging environments — business-hours support with a 4-hour response target. Tier 3 covers beta features and experimental services — best-effort support via GitHub issues with a 1-business-day response target.

Support requests arrive through defined channels: a dedicated Slack channel (#help-platform) for Tier 1 and Tier 2 issues where a bot auto-creates a Jira ticket, and a GitHub repository for Tier 3 feature requests and bug reports. The on-call engineer monitors PagerDuty for automated SLA breach alerts and the Slack channel for developer-reported issues.
SLA measurement

Availability is measured as (total minutes in period - minutes of platform unavailability) / total minutes, where unavailability is defined as the LLM gateway health check failing for 3 consecutive 1-minute polling intervals. Latency SLAs are measured at the platform API layer, not the upstream vendor — the platform owns the end-to-end response time including any retry logic.
Incident communication protocol

Every Tier 1 incident requires a communication cadence: initial acknowledgment within 15 minutes of detection, status updates every 30 minutes until resolution, and a postmortem published within 5 business days. The Statuspage should reflect each phase (investigating, identified, monitoring, resolved). This cadence should be documented in a runbook linked from the SLA page so that any on-call engineer can follow it without having to improvise during an incident.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Platform SLA tiers and support model: tier definitions, SLA metrics, and escalation paths

content.text: The SLA model defines three tiers with escalating response time commitments. Support flows through the Slack channel (Tier 1/2) or GitHub issues (Tier 3) into the ticketing system. Monitoring infrastructure (Grafana, PagerDuty, Statuspage) provides automated SLA breach detection and public status communication. The on-call rotation ensures 24x7 coverage for Tier 1 services.
When to use it
Platform launch: define the SLA before the first internal team onboards so their expectations are set correctly from day one.
Platform maturation: as the platform grows from internal tool to shared infrastructure, formalize the SLA at each maturity milestone.
Compliance requirements: some organizations require documented SLAs for any internal service that processes regulated data.
New Tier 1 consumer: when a new team onboards a production-critical use case, review and re-confirm the SLA commitments for that tier before they go live.
How to implement
Assess your current reliability: measure actual availability and latency for 4 weeks before publishing any SLA. Do not commit to targets you are currently missing.
Define tiers and map existing consumers: survey which developer teams are using the platform for production-critical vs. experimental use cases and assign tiers accordingly.
Draft the SLA document: availability target per tier, latency target per tier, support channel per tier, response time commitment per tier, and escalation path.
Build the measurement infrastructure: synthetic health checks, latency dashboards, PagerDuty alerts for SLA breach thresholds, and a public Statuspage.
Establish the on-call rotation: create a rotation schedule in PagerDuty that covers all Tier 1 production hours. Start with the platform team; expand to an extended rotation as the team grows.
Publish the SLA and communicate it: post the SLA to the engineering wiki, announce it in the platform channel, and reference it in all onboarding documentation.
Review and update quarterly: schedule a 30-minute SLA review at the end of each quarter to confirm targets are still achievable and tiers still match consumer use cases.
Examples

# internal-platform-sla.yaml
version: "1.2"
effective_date: "2025-07-01"

tiers:
  tier_1:
    name: "Production Critical"
    description: "Services in the revenue path or with regulatory commitments"
    availability_target: "99.9% per rolling 30 days"
    latency_target: "p99 < 3000ms at the platform API layer"
    support_channel: "#help-platform-critical (Slack)"
    response_time: "1 hour (24x7)"
    escalation:
      - "On-call engineer (PagerDuty)"
      - "Platform team lead (30 min if unacknowledged)"
      - "Engineering Director (1 hour if unresolved)"

  tier_2:
    name: "Standard"
    description: "Internal developer tooling, staging environments"
    availability_target: "99.5% during business hours (Mon-Fri 08:00-20:00 local)"
    latency_target: "p99 < 5000ms"
    support_channel: "#help-platform (Slack)"
    response_time: "4 business hours"
    escalation:
      - "On-call engineer (Jira ticket auto-created)"
      - "Team lead (next business day if unresolved)"

  tier_3:
    name: "Beta / Experimental"
    description: "Pre-release features, developer preview programs"
    availability_target: "Best effort, no SLA"
    support_channel: "GitHub Issues (platform-infra repo)"
    response_time: "1 business day"
Real-world example

A healthcare technology company's AI platform team was repeatedly pulled into ad-hoc escalations: developers messaging engineers directly when anything broke, no clear channel for help, and incidents being handled based on who was loudest rather than business impact. They published a three-tier SLA and support model over a two-week period.

The key outcome was not the document but the behavior change it enabled. Developers with Tier 1 production services now had a clear escalation path and a public Statuspage to check. The platform team's engineering manager used the SLA document to secure approval for an on-call rotation budget. Within one quarter, ad-hoc DMs to engineers dropped by 70%, and the platform team was able to reduce their incident response time from an average of 3.5 hours to 45 minutes because they had proper monitoring rather than relying on developer reports.
Common mistakes
Publishing SLAs before building the measurement infrastructure: you cannot track what you do not measure, and a missed SLA without a measurement system is invisible.
Starting too aggressive: committing to 99.99% availability before establishing baseline measurement almost always results in immediate SLA violations that damage trust.
Not including latency in the SLA: availability SLAs without latency commitments allow the platform to technically pass while being unusable (technically up but returning responses in 30 seconds).
Conflating support response time with resolution time: a 1-hour response time SLA means someone acknowledges the issue in 1 hour, not that it is resolved in 1 hour.
Letting tiers drift: as the platform evolves, some Tier 2 services become Tier 1 without the SLA being updated — resulting in missed commitments.
Best practices
Publish a public Statuspage (even if internal-only) so developers can check platform health without filing a ticket.
Conduct quarterly SLA reviews: confirm that current availability and latency match or exceed commitments, and update tiers as consumer use cases evolve.
Run monthly SLA drills: simulate a Tier 1 incident and measure actual response time against the committed target.
Make postmortems public within engineering: a blameless postmortem for every SLA breach builds more trust than silence.
Include SLA tiers in the platform onboarding documentation so developers self-select the appropriate tier before going live.
Performance and scalability

The operational overhead of a tiered SLA scales with the number of Tier 1 consumers, not total platform users. A platform with 5 Tier 1 services and 50 Tier 2 services can be operated with a single on-call engineer for Tier 1 coverage, with Tier 2 handled during business hours. As Tier 1 consumer count grows past 10-15 services, consider an extended on-call rotation with other engineering teams participating.

Monitoring infrastructure is a prerequisite, not optional. Synthetic health checks running every minute, latency histograms in Grafana, and PagerDuty alert thresholds for each SLA tier should be in place before the SLA is published. Without these, SLA compliance reporting relies on developer-reported incidents, which systematically undercounts violations.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
PagerDuty / OpsGenie
On-call routing and escalation
Set alert thresholds at 80% of SLA limit for early warning
Grafana / DataDog
SLA metric dashboards
Availability and latency with SLA threshold lines
Statuspage (Atlassian)
Public status communication
Internal Statuspage with per-tier service health
Jira / Linear
Support ticket tracking
Auto-create ticket from Slack bot for every #help-platform thread
Slack
Primary Tier 1/2 support channel
Dedicated #help-platform; bot routes to correct tier
GitHub Issues
Tier 3 feature requests and beta bugs
Lower overhead; searchable for deduplication
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Tiered SLA + support model
Clear expectations, accountable, scalable
Requires monitoring infra and on-call ops
Informal best-effort support
Zero overhead to set up
Erodes trust; creates inequitable access; no accountability
Single-tier SLA for all consumers
Simple to operate
Over-commits to low-priority use cases; under-commits to critical ones
External vendor SLA passthrough
No internal commitment overhead
Does not cover platform-layer issues; only covers upstream API
Community support model (async only)
Scales to many users without headcount
Unacceptable for production-critical use cases
Security and reliability

SLA measurement infrastructure must be treated as Tier 1 itself: if the monitoring system is down, SLA violations cannot be detected. Run the synthetic health checks from a separate infrastructure account or provider than the platform itself. Store SLA measurement data in an append-only audit log for regulatory and compliance review.

Support channels should not be used to share sensitive data: developers troubleshooting issues sometimes paste secrets, model outputs with PII, or internal data into Slack. Include a channel description warning and a Slack bot that scans for common secret patterns and auto-redacts or alerts.
Key takeaways
A platform SLA is a formal reliability and response-time commitment that transforms the platform from an informal tool into a product developers can build on with confidence.
Three tiers (production-critical, standard, beta) with differentiated response times and channels handle the full range of consumer use cases without over-committing resources.
Measure actual availability and latency for 4 weeks before publishing any SLA target — never commit to targets you are currently missing.
Build monitoring infrastructure (Grafana, PagerDuty, Statuspage) before publishing the SLA — a commitment you cannot measure is not a commitment.
Conduct quarterly SLA reviews and run monthly incident drills to ensure the operational posture can actually meet published commitments.
Postmortems for every SLA breach, published within engineering, build more trust than silence after a missed target.
The SLA tiers define what on-call coverage is required: each additional Tier 1 service added to the platform increases 24x7 on-call load.
